original_hypothesis.title: Proving Window Indefinitely Extended via Repeated Pause/Unpause by chain_pauser
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp. Both pauseProving(false) (via LibProving) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt. A chain_pauser who repeatedly toggles provingPaused (true->false) or the contract pause state resets lastUnpausedAt, extending the assigned prover's exclusive proving window indefinitely and preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If the assigned prover colludes with chain_pauser, they can monopolize the proving window indefinitely, preventing honest provers from submitting alternative proofs and potentially stalling the verification chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _checkProverPermission uses max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) for window calculation; pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt

preconditions: chain_pauser role is set (resolved via AddressManager)
Assigned prover intentionally does not prove within the original window

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibProving.sol lines 73-78 (pauseProving resets lastUnpausedAt) and lines 303-316 (_checkProverPermission window calc)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: lastUnpausedAt used as lower bound of proving window start; can be repeatedly reset
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No cap on how many times or how late lastUnpausedAt can be reset relative to block proposal time
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum proving window duration anchored to block proposal time

analysis_questions: How frequently can chain_pauser toggle the pause state?
Does the cooldown window in verification also use lastUnpausedAt, compounding the delay?
Can this be used to permanently prevent verification of specific blocks?
validation_requirements: Confirm that repeated pause/unpause resets lastUnpausedAt each time
Verify that max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) produces an ever-extending window
Test that non-assigned provers are permanently blocked while window is extended

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Block B proposed with assigned prover P at time T. 2) Proving window = T + 60 minutes (SGX tier). 3) P does not prove. 4) At T+59min, chain_pauser calls pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false), resetting lastUnpausedAt to T+59min. 5) New window: max(T, T+59min) + 60min = T+119min. 6) Repeat before each window expiry. 7) No other prover can ever submit proof for first transition of B.
prerequisites: Collusion between chain_pauser and assigned prover

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Block verification stalled; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users and honest provers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner removes chain_pauser role or upgrades contract

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for proving window extension via repeated pause toggling

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Two pauseProving calls per extension (~50K gas each)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring ProvingPaused events frequency

validation_result.mechanism: proving window extension via lastUnpausedAt reset
validation_result.impact_summary: A chain_pauser (or owner) can indefinitely extend the assigned prover's exclusive proving window by repeatedly toggling pauseProving(true)/pauseProving(false), preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions and stalling block verification.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability resides in the interaction between LibProving.pauseProving() and _checkProverPermission(). Each call to pauseProving(false) resets state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt to block.timestamp. The proving window calculation in _checkProverPermission uses max(_ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow * 60 >= block.timestamp. By repeatedly toggling pause on/off before the window expires, lastUnpausedAt is pushed forward, causing the max() to always pick the new lastUnpausedAt value, thus extending the window indefinitely. During the extended window, for tid==1 && ts.tier==0, only the assigned prover can submit a proof (non-assigned provers get L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER). If the assigned prover intentionally abstains, the block remains unprovable and unverifiable.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies LibProving.sol lines 73-78 for pauseProving and the proving window calculation. The pauseProving function at line 76 sets lastUnpausedAt = uint64(block.timestamp) when _pause is false. The _checkProverPermission function at lines 303-316 uses max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) + _tier.provingWindow * 60 >= block.timestamp. The combination of these two code paths enables the indefinite extension attack.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Each call with _pause=false resets lastUnpausedAt to current block.timestamp. The only guard is that the same value cannot be set twice consecutively (L1_INVALID_PAUSE_STATUS), but toggling true->false repeatedly resets lastUnpausedAt each time.

The proving window uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) as its start. Since lastUnpausedAt can be repeatedly pushed forward by pauseProving toggling, the window never expires. When inProvingWindow is true with tid==1 and ts.tier==0, only the assigned prover can prove - non-assigned provers are reverted with L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER.

calling_sequence: 2. chain_pauser calls TaikoL1.pauseProving(false) - sets provingPaused=false, lastUnpausedAt=block.timestamp (LibProving.sol:76)
3. Non-assigned prover tries TaikoL1.proveBlock() - passes whenProvingNotPaused (provingPaused is false)
4. LibProving._checkProverPermission evaluates: inProvingWindow = max(ts.timestamp, NEW_lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp → true
5. Since tid==1, ts.tier==0, inProvingWindow==true: revert L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER()
6. Repeat steps 1-2 before each window expiry to perpetually lock out other provers
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: TaikoL1.pauseProving(true) → _authorizePause(msg.sender) [passes for chain_pauser via onlyFromOwnerOrNamed] → LibProving.pauseProving(state, true) [sets provingPaused=true]. Then TaikoL1.pauseProving(false) → LibProving.pauseProving(state, false) [sets provingPaused=false AND lastUnpausedAt=block.timestamp at line 76]. When any non-assigned prover calls proveBlock → LibProving.proveBlock → _checkProverPermission at line 307: inProvingWindow = max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) + _tier.provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp. Since lastUnpausedAt was just reset to near-current time, the window is effectively restarted. For tid==1 && ts.tier==0 && inProvingWindow, only assigned prover is allowed (line 312-313).

deployment_assumptions_checked: chain_pauser role is set via AddressManager
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment scripts (DeployOnL1.s.sol) do not explicitly register a chain_pauser address. However, the owner can always perform this attack regardless (onlyFromOwnerOrNamed allows owner). The AddressManager's setAddress can be used post-deployment to set chain_pauser. Even without chain_pauser, the owner (timelock/security council) retains the same capability.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid: 1) Block proposed with assigned prover P at time T. 2) ts.timestamp initialized to blk.proposedAt=T in _createTransition (LibProving.sol line 242). 3) Proving window = max(T, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60. 4) Before window expires, chain_pauser calls pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false), resetting lastUnpausedAt to near-current time. 5) New window = max(T, T+~3599) + 3600 = T+~7199. 6) This is repeatable indefinitely. 7) Non-assigned provers always hit L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER because inProvingWindow remains true with tid==1 and ts.tier==0. The only constraint is the chain_pauser must toggle before each window expiry (~every provingWindow minutes).
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. The attack prevents verification of blocks whose assigned provers collude with chain_pauser. Since verification is sequential (lastVerifiedBlockId can only increase contiguously via LibVerifying.verifyBlocks), blocking one block halts the entire verification chain. All subsequent blocks cannot be verified. Prover bonds are locked. L2 chain finality stalls. Cross-chain state syncing via SignalService stops. The attack costs minimal gas (~100K per toggle cycle) and has no capital requirement beyond gas costs.
recommended_mitigations: Add an absolute maximum proving window cap anchored to the block's proposedAt timestamp, e.g.: if (block.timestamp > _blk.proposedAt + MAX_PROVING_WINDOW) then treat the window as expired regardless of lastUnpausedAt
Add a cooldown period between pauseProving toggles to prevent rapid cycling
Use a separate proving window extension mechanism that caps the total extension rather than resetting lastUnpausedAt on each unpause
Consider allowing guardian-tier proofs to bypass the proving window check entirely (they already bypass via contestBond==0)
Emit monitoring events and implement on-chain rate limiting for pause/unpause operations
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TaikoL1 with a chain_pauser set in AddressManager. 2. Propose a block with assigned prover P. 3. Wait until near window expiry. 4. As chain_pauser, call pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false). 5. Attempt proveBlock from a different address - expect L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER revert. 6. Verify that the window has been extended by checking that the revert persists past the original window expiry. 7. Repeat steps 3-6 to confirm indefinite extension.
uncertainties: Whether chain_pauser is actually configured in production deployments (deployment scripts don't set it, but the owner can always perform the same attack)
Whether the collusion scenario between chain_pauser and assigned prover is realistic in practice given the role is likely held by a multisig or governance
The guardian tier can always bypass this (contestBond==0 returns early in _checkProverPermission), so guardians provide an escape hatch
additional_testing_needed: Forge test that toggles pauseProving and verifies the proving window extends for non-assigned provers
Test that confirms verification stalls when a single block's proving window is kept extended
Test the interaction between TaikoL1.unpause() (full contract unpause) and lastUnpausedAt reset
Test that guardian proofs can still bypass the extended window as an escape mechanism
